items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: Why did the League of Nations fail to prevent WWII?
items.correct: Absence of the US, requirement of unanimous decisions, no armed force of its own, weak responses to Manchuria (1931) and Abyssinia (1935), and great-power self-interest.
items.misconception: The League failed because no countries joined it.
items.random_wrong: The League failed because Hitler banned it in 1933.
items.why: Many countries joined; the failure was structural and political, not absence. Students reduce complex institutional failure to a single fact.

items.q: Did the Treaty of Versailles directly cause WWII?
items.correct: It contributed via German resentment and economic strain, but the 1930s Depression, Hitler's choices, and Allied appeasement were equally necessary — Versailles alone is not sufficient.
items.misconception: Versailles caused WWII directly because it was so harsh on Germany.
items.random_wrong: Versailles caused WWII because it required Germany to invade Poland.
items.why: Comparable peace terms have not always led to war. Modern historians stress contingency; treating Versailles as sufficient cause is a simple but inadequate story.

items.q: When was the Munich Agreement signed?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 29 September 1938. “1 September 1939” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which pact in August 1939 cleared the way for German invasion of Poland?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nazi-Soviet Pact. “Anti-Comintern Pact” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On what day in September 1939 did Germany invade Poland?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
